svg.title: SecureGuard language support — what works today vs what needs adding
svg.desc: Matrix showing Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, React, Angular, and Java across five capability dimensions: scanner, Semgrep rules, registry, dependency scan, and production readiness.

g.onclick: sendPrompt('What scanners does SecureGuard use for Python?')
sendPrompt('Is Python fully supported in SecureGuard today?')
sendPrompt('What scanners does SecureGuard use for TypeScript?')
sendPrompt('Is TypeScript fully supported in SecureGuard today?')
sendPrompt('What does SecureGuard detect in React codebases?')
sendPrompt('What Semgrep rules does React need added to SecureGuard?')
sendPrompt('What does SecureGuard detect in Angular codebases?')
sendPrompt('What rules does Angular need added to SecureGuard?')
sendPrompt('What needs to be added to SecureGuard to support Java?')
sendPrompt('How long would it take to add Java support to SecureGuard?')

svg.text: — core engine (server, Claude, registry, audit, policy gate) works for all languages unchanged
The entire SecureGuard backend — Express server, Claude remediator, policy gate, audit log, PR bot — is
completely language-agnostic. Adding a new language means: (1) add a scanner subprocess, (2) add Semgrep
YAML rules, (3) add registry entries. Nothing else changes. React and Angular work today for XSS + secrets.
